title: Truth Sources
subtitle: 429 Research · Thesis Note

verdict.thesis: AI agents don't kill the system of record — they clock into it. CRMs and ledgers are the truth sources agents read from and write to, and the vendors are becoming metered resellers of frontier-model tokens. The market is pricing these franchises as AI roadkill; we think a subset of them are AI toll roads. Watchlist, not positions — the entry prices and the triggers are at the bottom.

stances.name: HubSpot
stances.line: −78% from high, ~15× forward earnings, ~11× free cash flow. The credits engine is real but not yet in the numbers. We want proof net-new ARR has bottomed — or a panic print.

stances.name: Salesforce
stances.line: Cheapest in its public history at ~13× forward. $1.2B Agentforce ARR growing 205%. Slower core, bigger buyback. The safer expression of the same thesis.

stances.name: Xero
stances.line: Fundamentals accelerating into the fear: FY27 guided +31–35%, ~1% monthly churn, street average target ~A$130 with zero sells. Earnings optics are Melio-depressed — we want H1 FY27 proof the US bet is landing, or another visit to the June panic lows.

sections.label: The Idea
sections.title: No such thing as guerrilla accounting
sections.body:
There is guerrilla marketing. There is no guerrilla accounting, and no guerrilla sales pipeline. Tax has to be filed the same way whether you are a barber or a biotech; a deal moves from lead to close through the same stages everywhere. Because these workflows barely vary between businesses, the software that runs them gets enormous economies of scale — millions of customers funding one product — and the customers get enormous switching costs, because the software holds their history, their compliance trail, and their money.

That is why we don't believe the CRMs and the ledgers get replaced by AI. They are **data spaces — truth sources**. An agent can draft an email or write a report from anywhere, but the moment it needs to know *what is actually true* about a customer, an invoice, or a bank balance — and the moment it needs to *change* that truth with permissions and an audit trail — it has to go where the record lives.

The second half of the idea: the agents will be **staff**. They will spend their working day inside Xero, Salesforce and HubSpot the way employees do today, except they'll be metered. Every action an agent takes inside these platforms burns tokens from Anthropic or OpenAI underneath, and the platform bills the customer in credits on top. As long as the incumbents stay open — plug into every model provider, every agent surface — they effectively become **resellers of frontier-model tokens**, with the markup that resellers earn.

This note tests that idea against what actually happened in 2025–26, then puts numbers on three names the market has thrown out: the two CRMs — and the ledger that fits our own book.

sections.label: The Stack
sections.title: Where the agent clocks in
sections.note: The system of record sits between the model labs and the business: it buys intelligence wholesale (tokens) and sells work retail (credits, resolutions, seats). Its moat is not the UI — it's the data, the permissions, and the compliance rails agents can't fake.

layers.title: Frontier models
layers.desc: Anthropic · OpenAI — intelligence, sold wholesale by the token

layers.title: Systems of record — the truth source
layers.desc: Salesforce · HubSpot · Xero — the data, the permissions, the audit trail, the workflow rails
items: MCP servers: the tool layer agents call
Compliance: tax filing, GAAP, payroll rails

layers.title: Agents as staff
layers.desc: Metered digital labor, billed in credits and outcomes
items: Sold against labor budgets, not software seats

sections.label: The Evidence
sections.title: The agents already clocked in
sections.note: This stopped being a forecast in 2025. Machine traffic passed human traffic on the open web in June 2026, and the metered-agent revenue lines are compounding triple-digit.

cards.x: cumulative through Jan 2026 — Agentforce runs on Anthropic & OpenAI models underneath
+205% y/y at Q1 FY27 (May 2026) — fastest-scaling product line Salesforce has reported
quarter-over-quarter growth in credits consumed, Q1 2026
automated requests passed human traffic on Cloudflare Radar, June 2026; agentic traffic +7,851% y/y (HUMAN Security)

sections.label: The De-Rating
sections.title: The market has already voted the other way
sections.body:
Every one of these franchises trades like it is being disrupted *now*. Nadella said business applications "collapse in the agent era." Klarna ripped out Salesforce to prove it — then admitted it hadn't replaced SaaS with an LLM at all, and rehired the humans. In February 2026, Claude Cowork and OpenAI Frontier launched in the same week and took roughly a trillion dollars off software in days. The sector's median revenue multiple roughly halved.

The result is the chart below. HubSpot has lost more than three-quarters of its value from the February 2025 high. Salesforce is the second-worst Dow stock of 2026 at the cheapest forward multiple in its public history. Xero — accelerating, 48.5 Rule of 40 — is down ~60% from where it placed stock in mid-2025.

De-ratings this violent are usually either the market correctly smelling terminal decline, or the entry point of the decade. The rest of this note is about telling those apart.

sections.label: Drawdown
sections.title: Drawdown from 2025 highs
sections.note: HUBS from $881.13 all-time high (Feb 13, 2025) to $192.12 (Jul 2, 2026). CRM from $276.80 52-week high to $166.11. XRO from its A$196.52 all-time high (Jun 24, 2025 — the day before the Melio placement at A$176) to A$72.25 (Jul 3, 2026).

events.text: Nadella on BG2: business applications "collapse in the agent era" — SaaS is just CRUD plus logic, and the logic moves to agents.
Klarna "shuts down Salesforce" … then admits it didn't replace SaaS with an LLM, swaps Workday for Deel, and rehires human support after quality fell. The bear case's poster child recants.
Dreamforce: Anthropic and OpenAI deals — Claude becomes a foundational model inside Agentforce 360; GPT-5 native; Agentforce apps inside ChatGPT. The record opens up instead of walling off.
Claude Cowork and OpenAI Frontier launch the same week. "Trillion-dollar" software selloff; median software multiple roughly halves. HUBS, CRM, XRO all crater.
HubSpot flips agents to outcome pricing — $0.50 per resolved conversation, pay only when the agent completes the task. Second leg of the software selloff.
HUBS Q1: net-new ARR growth falls below revenue growth for the first time → downgrade cascade (BofA PT $180). Founder Dharmesh Shah personally buys the dip.
Cloudflare: automated traffic passes human traffic. Guggenheim upgrades CRM — the "AI Armageddon" priced into software is "a hallucination." Xero's JAX embeds into Microsoft 365 Copilot.

sections.label: The Moat
sections.title: Why the record survives
sections.body:
Three pieces of evidence carry most of the weight.

### The customers don't leave

Xero loses about 1% of subscribers a month. QuickBooks Online renews at ~82–88% depending on tier. HubSpot's net revenue retention is ~103% and *improving*; Salesforce attrition is stable at ~8% a year. None of these look like products being replaced — and this is 18 months into the loudest disruption narrative software has ever had.

### The challengers keep hitting the same wall

Bench — $100M+ of venture money for AI bookkeeping — imploded over Christmas 2024; the AI "didn't work properly." Digits' autonomous ledger serves hundreds of businesses against Xero's 4.6 million and QuickBooks' ~10 million. Day.ai, the Sequoia-backed "Cursor of CRM" aimed straight at HubSpot, has on the order of a hundred customers. The AI-native winners so far — Clay at $3.1B, Basis at $1.15B — sell *tools that sit on top of* the record, they don't replace it. The pattern is consistent: generic intelligence is easy to get now; the trusted, permissioned, compliance-wired record is not.

### Even the walk-backs prove it

Klarna is the cleanest natural experiment anyone ran. A motivated, technically strong company publicly tried to delete its CRM in favor of AI — and ended up on other SaaS, a knowledge graph, and rehired humans, with the CEO explicitly doubting others should try. The lesson isn't that AI failed; it's that the record was never the part AI replaces.

The honest caveat: stickiness protects the *stock* only if the seats it bills for survive. The real bear case was never rip-and-replace — it's slow seat compression while credits ramp too slowly to offset. That's the race the deep dives below try to handicap.

sections.label: Token Resellers
sections.title: The token resale business, priced
sections.body:
Every incumbent has now built the same machine: buy intelligence wholesale from the labs, sell work retail to the customer. The pricing tells you what the machine earns. An Agentforce "action" is capped around ten thousand tokens — pennies at API rates — and retails at $0.10. HubSpot retails a resolved support conversation at $0.50 against a human cost of $20–50 per ticket. That's the resale spread: order-of-magnitude markups on the token cost at list price, sold against **labor budgets, not software budgets**.

Two things keep this honest. First, nobody discloses AI gross margin yet — Bessemer pegs AI-heavy software at ~60–70% gross margin versus 80–90% classic SaaS, so the spread is real but thinner than old software. Second, the deflation is deliberate: Salesforce went $2 per conversation → $0.10 per action → pay-per-resolution in eighteen months; HubSpot halved its agent price in April. They are racing prices down to make agents a no-brainer — margin now traded for metered volume later. That's the right trade if, and only if, agent volume compounds the way usage metrics say it is.

sections.label: HubSpot
sections.title: HubSpot — the cheap shot at the thesis
sections.body: The market's single most hated large-cap software stock, and the purest expression of the fear: SMB customers, seat pricing, and a marketing wedge that ChatGPT supposedly eats. Down 78% while growing 18% with expanding margins.

cards.x: all-time high $881.13, Feb 13 2025
on FY26 non-GAAP EPS guide of $12.38–12.46
market cap ~$9.8B, ~$1.8B net cash
~$740M FCF guided for 2026
+16% y/y; NRR ~103% and improving
FY26 guide — hit the 2027 target a year early
the number that makes it screen expensive — see below

sections.label: HUBS Agents
sections.title: Breeze agents: customers activating, quarter over quarter
sections.note: Customers with each agent live. Customer Agent resolves 70% of conversations on average (some >90%) twelve months after launch; total credits consumed grew +67% q/q in Q1 2026. Dollar revenue from credits: still undisclosed — the honest hole in the bull case.

sections.label: HUBS Bull/Bear
sections.title: The two stories
bull: Distribution: 300k SMBs get turnkey agents with their CRM context attached — HubSpot is how small businesses that can't build AI, buy AI.
Outcome pricing sells against labor: $0.50 per resolved ticket vs $20–50 human cost. TAM stops being 'seats at SMBs' and starts being 'SMB payroll'.
First CRM connected into both ChatGPT (Jun 2025) and Claude (Jul 2025), plus a production MCP server — the truth source for whatever agent surface wins.
Franchise health: margins at target a year early, NRR improving, founder buying the dip with his own money.
At ~15× forward / ~11× FCF, you're paying a GDP-growth multiple for a mid-teens grower with an un-modeled consumption line.
bear: Net-new ARR growth fell below revenue growth in Q1 2026 — the leading indicator went the wrong way, and the downgrade cascade followed.
ARPU is flat (+2pts cc): all growth is volume at the Starter tier. If agents shrink GTM headcount, the seat base erodes from the top.
The credits line is real but undisclosed — management won't put a dollar number on AI revenue, which means it doesn't yet move the P&L.
ChatGPT genuinely absorbs the content/email/SEO jobs that were HubSpot's inbound wedge; AEO is a defensive crouch.
Outcome pricing is self-deflating: the agent price was cut 50% in April. Volume has to outrun deflation forever.

sections.label: Salesforce
sections.title: Salesforce — the toll road, already tolling
sections.body: The strongest version of the truth-source argument isn't a forecast — it's on the income statement. $1.2B of Agentforce ARR, 20 trillion tokens through the platform, Claude and GPT-5 serving inside its trust boundary. Priced at the cheapest forward multiple in its public history.

cards.x: 52-week high $276.80; worst-2 Dow stock of 2026
sources span 12–15×; cheapest in its public history
+11% y/y (~3pts from Informatica)
Q1 FY27; FCF $14.4B in FY26
+205% y/y; AI + Data ARR $3.4B (~7% of revenue)
cRPO $33.6B, +14% — the franchise isn't shrinking
buyback authorization + $25B accelerated repurchase (debt-funded)
revenue attrition, stable through the whole AI panic

sections.label: Agentforce ARR
sections.title: Agentforce ARR: zero to $1.2B in six quarters
sections.note: Disclosed Agentforce ARR by quarter since launch (Oct 2024). The caveat that keeps us honest: Stifel estimates only ~5% of Salesforce customers have adopted, and ~1/3 of cumulative deals are paid — it's a fast ramp off a base the bears call rounding error (~2.6% of revenue).

sections.label: CRM Bull/Bear
sections.title: The two stories
bull: The reseller machine at scale: ~20T tokens processed, billed back as $0.10 actions, $125–550 seats and per-resolution fees. Fastest-scaling product line the company has ever reported.

Both labs inside the tent: Claude is a foundational model within Agentforce 360 (preferred for regulated industries), GPT-5 native, Agentforce apps inside ChatGPT. Co-opetition beats disintermediation.
Benioff's own layoffs are the demo: support headcount 9,000 → 5,000 with AI handling half of interactions — digital labor sold with receipts.
$14–16B FCF, 34%+ margins, $50B buyback at ~13× forward: the downside case is being paid to wait.
Guggenheim's Jul 1 upgrade framed it: current multiples price software 'declining into perpetuity' — any outcome short of Armageddon re-rates it.
bear: Core growth is ~10–11% and 3 points of it is acquired (Informatica). AI + Data is ~7% of revenue — triple-digit growth on a base that can't move the needle for years.
The seat-cannibalization math is unproven: consumption revenue may replace, not add to, $125-seat revenue. Salesforce cutting its own headcount proves agents kill seats too.
FCF growth was cut to +4–5% this year to fund a debt-financed buyback — financial engineering where organic growth used to be.
Claude-in-Slack and OpenAI Frontier both raise the same question: if the assistant layer lives elsewhere, Agentforce becomes plumbing priced like plumbing.
Adoption reality: <10% of the install base has signed any Agentforce deal; most deals are pilots, not production.

sections.label: Xero
sections.title: Xero — the ledger version, growing fastest
sections.body: The accounting expression of the thesis, and the strangest chart of the three: the fundamentals accelerated all the way through the panic. Revenue grew 31% in FY26 and is guided to grow 31–35% in FY27, churn is ~1% a month, and JAX — powered by Claude — has already reconciled 40M+ bank transactions at 97% accuracy and ships inside Microsoft 365 Copilot from August. Meanwhile the stock fell 63% from its June 2025 high and briefly traded at COVID-era prices. It's also the one that fits the AUD book natively.

sections.label: XRO Vitals
sections.title: The vitals
sections.note: Xero reports in NZ$ with a March fiscal year-end; the share price is A$. FY27 guidance given May 14, 2026: revenue NZ$3.6–3.7B, adjusted EBITDA NZ$860–920M, including up to NZ$55M of extra US brand spend.

cards.x: all-time high A$196.52 (Jun 24, 2025); June 2026 panic low ~A$65 — COVID-era prices
market cap ~A$12.5B; EV ~5.0× FY26 revenue of NZ$2.75B

sections.label: XRO Growth
sections.title: Accelerating into the fear: revenue growth by fiscal year
sections.note: Fiscal years end March 31, reported in NZ$. FY26 includes ~5 months of Melio (organic +21%); FY27E is the guidance midpoint (NZ$3.6–3.7B). Trailing P/E screens at ~80× because Melio acquisition and amortization costs cut NPAT 27% — the same GAAP-optics trap as HubSpot, in a different costume.

sections.label: XRO Bull/Bear
sections.title: The two stories
bull: The deepest compliance moat in the note: tax filing (BAS, MTD), payroll and bank feeds are wired into the ledger — the workflows with literally zero tolerance for a hallucination.

JAX is working at scale: 40M+ transactions reconciled at 97% accuracy, usage +61% in three months, messages per user +115%. Claude powers it, XeroForce lets customers build their own agents, and from August JAX lives inside Microsoft 365 Copilot — Xero data on every agent surface.

AI is monetized as pricing power today (ARPC +23%, churn ~1%/month, 2.6M customers already using AI features) — and a metered layer is coming: management starts AI monetization in FY27, mixing bundling, à-la-carte and usage-linked pricing, with the CFO saying token costs won't materially dent gross margin.
Melio makes it the 'active GL' — accounting plus payments plus payroll — with US core revenue up 240% in the first partial year.
Rule of 40 at 48.5 and rising, a fresh A$550M buyback, and a street that is unanimous the wrong way for a disrupted business: 14 buys, zero sells, ~A$130 average target.
bear: The screen trap is real until it isn't: ~80× trailing earnings, net debt of NZ$383M plus up to US$500M of Melio earnout — the market can keep pricing the optics for a long time.

Xero has failed in the US before. Melio is a US$2.5B+ second attempt that cut group gross margin 5.1pts to 83.9% (Melio runs at ~35–45%), isn't guided to break even until H2 FY28, and needs NZ$55M of extra brand spend — and ex-Melio, North American subscribers actually slipped in FY26.

The bank-data-fee risk went from theory to invoice: JPMorgan now has paid data contracts covering >95% of aggregator pulls, and the CFPB's open-banking rule is enjoined. RBC sized broad adoption at up to −27% of EBITDA worst case — live and not in guidance.
AI-native bookkeeping (Digits, Puzzle, Fondo) attacks exactly this: SMB books are the most template-able workflow in software. Sub-scale today; aimed directly here.
Because AI is bundled, there is no consumption revenue line — if agents do become the economy, Xero captures it only through price rises, not a meter.

sections.label: Screen vs Read
sections.title: What the screen sees, and what it misses
sections.body:
The valuation screen that prompted this note scores HubSpot 2/6 — "expensive" — and Salesforce 4/6, mostly on trailing P/E. That's the machine reading GAAP optics. HubSpot's 98× trailing P/E is an artifact of stock-comp accounting on barely-positive GAAP earnings; on guided cash earnings it's ~15×, and on free cash flow ~11×. Salesforce screens at 17× trailing because its GAAP earnings are real — the screen and the read agree it's cheap.

Where the screen is useful: both names trade far below its DCF fair values ($820 for HUBS, $300 for CRM). We wouldn't underwrite $820 — a DCF on a disrupted-narrative stock is a fistfight between terminal assumptions — but the direction matches our read: the market is pricing terminal decline into franchises whose customers aren't leaving.

The growth-multiple picture below is the cleaner way to see it: both companies now carry market-or-lower forward multiples while growing faster than the market.

sections.label: P/E Reality
sections.title: Trailing optics vs forward reality
sections.note: Trailing P/E as screened (Simply Wall St, GAAP). Forward P/E on FY26 guided non-GAAP EPS (HUBS ~$12.42; CRM consensus ~$12.5–13). Reference lines: US software industry trailing average 27.7×, US market 19.2×. Same company, different accounting lens — an 83-point gap for HubSpot.

sections.label: Growth
sections.title: Revenue growth: decelerating, not dying
sections.note: Annual revenue growth by fiscal year (HubSpot calendar FY; Salesforce FY ends January, plotted against the closest calendar year; CRM FY27E includes ~3pts from Informatica). The bear case needs these lines to go to zero; the bull case just needs them to stabilize.

sections.label: Scenarios
sections.title: Three ways each story ends
sections.note: Illustrative 2–3 year scenarios. HUBS and CRM: forward non-GAAP EPS × an exit multiple, anchored to the pasted screen's fair values where relevant. XRO: EV/sales, because earnings are Melio-depressed (trailing P/E ~80× is an artifact). These are sizing tools, not predictions.

marks.label: Base

marks.label: Bull

marks.label: Bull

sections.label: Triggers & Risks
sections.title: What would make us buy — and what kills the thesis
sections.body:
No position in either name today. What moves them onto the book:

### Buy triggers — HubSpot

- **Net-new ARR growth re-crosses above revenue growth**, or two consecutive quarters of stabilization. That was the metric that broke the stock; it's the metric that fixes it.
- **A dollar disclosure on credits.** The first quarter management quantifies AI/credit revenue, the un-modeled line becomes modelable — and likely gets bought hard.
- **A panic print into the $140s–150s** (~12× forward) on no fundamental change — paying a market multiple for the franchise with the option thrown in free.

### Buy triggers — Salesforce

- **Organic (ex-Informatica) growth holding ≥9%** while Agentforce compounds — evidence consumption is additive, not substitutive.
- **Agentforce ARR through $2B with paid-deal share rising.** At that run-rate AI stops being rounding error.
- The stock already sits near its bear-case floor; below ~$150 the buyback does the underwriting for us.

### Buy triggers — Xero

- **H1 FY27 (November 2026): organic growth ≥20% with Melio tracking to its H2 FY28 breakeven.** The NPAT optics trough passing is the re-rating catalyst.
- **The FY27 AI monetization rollout.** Management has said metered AI pricing starts this year — the first evidence customers pay it converts JAX from a cost line into the thesis.
- **A re-test of the June lows (~A$65–66, ~3.4× FY27E sales)** buys the FY28 revenue double at half the street's targets — that print, we don't wait for the November result.

### What kills the thesis

- **Seat compression shows up in the reported numbers** — HubSpot ARPU turning negative, Salesforce cRPO growth breaking below ~10% — while credit lines stay small. That's the race being lost, not feared.
- **The labs go direct at the record.** If Anthropic or OpenAI ship a genuine system-of-record product (not an assistant surface) and win real migrations, the truth-source moat is breached. Watch OpenAI Frontier's customer list, not its press releases.
- **AI gross margin turns out structurally thin.** If metered agent revenue lands at 40–50% gross margin instead of 60–70%, the reseller spread was a mirage and these deserve their de-rated multiples.
- **A churn inflection.** The whole thesis rests on ~1%/month and NRR >100%. If stickiness cracks anywhere in the cohort data, exit first, re-underwrite later.

sections.label: Conclusion
sections.title: Conclusion — the options on the table
sections.intro: One thesis, three expressions, three very different setups. Salesforce is the thesis already on the income statement at the cheapest multiple in its history. HubSpot is the thesis at maximum pessimism, waiting on one metric to turn. Xero is the thesis accelerating in plain sight, priced as if it were dying. All three trade at a fraction of their 2025 highs for the same single reason — and if that reason is wrong, we don't need to pick the perfect ticket. These are the ways to play it:

options.name: Do nothing yet
options.body: Wait until the credits/consumption lines are disclosed dollars and the seat-compression question is settled. Cleanest information, worst prices — every trigger in this note resolving positively is also the event that takes the discount away.
options.fit: Cost: certainty is the most expensive thing on this page.

options.name: Start with Salesforce
options.body: The conservative core: ~13× forward with $14B+ of free cash flow, a $50B buyback underwriting the floor, and the agent thesis already producing $1.2B of ARR. Least torque of the three (+30% base case), least dependence on anything going right.
options.fit: For capital that wants the thesis without the drama.

options.name: Wait for HubSpot's signal
options.body: The torque: −78% and ~15× forward, but the leading indicator (net-new ARR) is still pointing down. Buy the stabilization print, the first credits dollar-disclosure, or a panic into the $140s–150s — not before.
options.fit: Highest upside in the note (bull case +116%) — earned by waiting.

options.name: Xero on proof or panic
options.body: The AUD-native ticket with the best fundamentals of the three and two clean entry points: H1 FY27 in November showing Melio on its FY28 breakeven path, or a re-test of the A$65–66 June lows. The FY27 AI-monetization rollout is the free option on top.
options.fit: Fits the AUD book; November 2026 is the decision date.

options.name: Staged basket across all three
options.body: What we'd actually do: open a starter position in Salesforce now, then add HubSpot and Xero as their triggers fire — targeting a full thesis weight built across two or three entries rather than one guess. The bet is the thesis (the record survives and meters the agents), not any single ticker; staging caps the cost of being early and keeps us exposed if the whole complex re-rates first.
options.fit: Fits the Conviction sleeve: thesis-driven, owned outright, no leverage, entries earned not guessed.

sections.outro: Discipline notes: if the complex re-rates before the triggers arrive, we let most of it go — the starter position is the hedge against that regret. And the thesis gets falsified by cohort data, not headlines: churn, NRR, cRPO and net-new ARR are the four numbers we re-read every quarter. The moment stickiness cracks, this whole note is wrong and we exit first, re-underwrite later.

sections.label: Sources
sections.title: Sources & method
sections.note: Compiled July 5–6, 2026 from company filings and press releases, earnings-call coverage, and the reporting below. Prices as of July 2–3, 2026. The Simply Wall St screen (fair values, PE/PEG/PB comparisons) was supplied to us and is quoted as 'the screen' throughout. Xero reports in NZ$ with a March year-end; its share price and targets are A$. Key figures were cross-checked across at least two sources; numbers we could not verify are flagged in the text.

disclaimer: 429 Capital is a private personal investment record maintained for the owner's own use. This research note is not an offer, a solicitation, or financial advice, and does not take anyone's objectives or circumstances into account. Figures are compiled from public sources as of July 5–6, 2026 and may contain errors; scenario values are illustrative arithmetic, not forecasts. No position is held in HUBS, CRM or XRO at the date of writing. Past performance is not indicative of future performance.
